blocks.content: The waiting room was beige and fluorescent and entirely indifferent to the fact that it had held the end of several worlds tonight.
They arrived in stages — her parents first, then Maya and Nathan, then Lena who had driven separately because she was the only one who had remembered to lock the house.

Ethan was already there. He was in a chair against the wall with his elbows on his knees and his hands locked together and his eyes on the floor. He had been sitting exactly like that since they had taken her through the doors.
He looked up when Thomas Hailey walked in.
Something passed between them — just a look, barely a second — and then Thomas crossed the room and sat beside him.

Maya sat across from them. Nathan beside her. She was holding his hand with both of hers and looking at nothing — the wall, the floor, somewhere between the two. She had the look of someone who had been bracing for impact their entire life and had just been hit and was still working out why it still felt like a surprise.

Her mother sat near the window. She was praying. Not ostentatiously — just quietly, lips barely moving, hands in her lap. The way she had probably prayed every night for twenty-two years without anyone knowing she was doing it.
Lena brought coffee from a machine down the hall. Nobody asked. Nobody refused.
